Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.

After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The board changed. Let me check where the dot moved to. It was at (28, 25) before, and I clicked on (29, 26) which is the bottom-right cell. Let me see if it moved in a different direction.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Magenta dots:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 4 and n['id'] > 3:
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
print("White bar:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W':
        print(f"  pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The dot is still at (28, 25)! It didn't move. But the white bar went from 20 to 18, so the action did cost a timer unit.
Wait, that's strange. The board changed (board_changed: true), but the dot didn't move. Let me check what changed.
Actually, looking more carefully, the dot boundary is [[28, 25], [28, 26], [29, 26], [29, 25]], which is the same as before. So the dot didn't move.
But the board_changed was true. Maybe something else changed, like the white bar.
Hmm, this is confusing. Let me check if the dot actually moved but the boundary is the same because the dot is 2x2 and the boundary is the outer corners.
Actually, wait. The boundary [[28, 25], [28, 26], [29, 26], [29, 25]] represents a 2x2 square with top-left at (28, 25) and bottom-right at (29, 26). This is the same as before. So the dot didn't move.
But I clicked on (29, 26) which is part of the dot. Maybe clicking on the bottom-right cell doesn't move the dot, or maybe it moves it in a different direction.
Actually, let me reconsider. Previously, when I clicked on (28, 25) (top-left), the dot moved from (29, 26) to (28, 25). Now I clicked on (29, 26) (bottom-right), and the dot stayed at (28, 25).
Wait, that doesn't make sense. Let me trace the dot's position history:
1. Initial position after merging: (39, 36) - this was the right-side dot (id=10 originally)
2. After 10 clicks: (29, 26) - moved up-left 10 times
3. After 1 more click on (29, 26): (28, 25) - moved up-left 1 more time
4. After clicking on (29, 26) again: still at (28, 25) - didn't move!
So the dot is now at (28, 25), and I clicked on (29, 26) which is now outside the dot (the dot is at rows 28-29, cols 25-26). Wait, (29, 26) is still part of the dot! The dot's cells are (28, 25), (28, 26), (29, 25), (29, 26).
So I clicked on (29, 26) which is the bottom-right cell of the dot. But the dot didn't move. Maybe clicking on different cells of the dot makes it move in different directions?
Let me test this by clicking on other cells of the dot.
Actually, wait. The dot didn't move, but the board changed. Maybe the white bar changed from 20 to 18, which is the board change.
Let me try clicking on a different cell of the dot, like (28, 26) (top-right).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try clicking on different cells of the same dot to see if direction changes
# Dot cells: (28,25)=TL, (28,26)=TR, (29,25)=BL, (29,26)=BR
# Previously clicked TL (28,25) -> moved up-left
# Then clicked BR (29,26) -> didn't move (or maybe moved differently?)
# Let me try TR (28,26)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 28, 'col': 26}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
